Original Persona: A devout Catholic grandmother who prays for the seminarian's vocation and provides unwavering support
Expanded Persona:
María is a devout Hispanic Catholic grandmother, born in 1937, who prays daily for the seminarian’s vocation and offers unwavering spiritual and emotional support. She attends Mass regularly, keeps a well-worn rosary by her bedside, and lights candles for his intentions at her parish. Having lived through many decades of change in the Church and in the world, she values tradition, humility, and perseverance in the face of hardship. She often writes encouraging notes, shares stories of the faith that sustained her family, and reminds him to trust in God’s providence. Though her health has slowed her a bit, she remains steadfast in her intercession and sees supporting his vocation as one of her most important callings in this season of her life.

03/21/1955.Event: On her 18th birthday, María begins a full‑time position as a seamstress at Casa de Modas Rivera, a small dressmaking shop, helping support her parents while appreciating the quiet, repetitive work that allows her to meditate and pray the rosary silently during breaks.

Anchors.tool_or_platform: Singer 201K sewing machine

09/15/1956.Event: María begins attending daily Mass before work at St. Cecilia Catholic Church, forming a lifelong habit of early morning worship and Eucharistic devotion that she prefers over social gatherings or late-night outings.

Anchors.tool_or_platform: 1962 Daily Roman Missal

05/13/1957.Event: On the feast of Our Lady of Fatima, María joins the parish’s Legion of Mary prayer group, embracing traditional Marian devotions and committing to weekly door-to-door visitations despite disliking confrontational or aggressive evangelization styles.

Anchors.org: Legion of Mary, St. Cecilia Presidium
Anchors.tool_or_platform: Legion of Mary Handbook

10/04/1958.Event: After months of courtship centered around parish events, María marries a fellow Hispanic Catholic, José, at a nuptial Mass, pledging to build a family rooted in traditional faith practices rather than modern secular trends that she already finds unsettling.

Anchors.tool_or_platform: Pre-Cana marriage preparation booklet

01/06/1960.Event: María gives birth to her first child on the feast of Epiphany, vowing to raise her children with nightly family rosaries and stories of the faith that sustained her immigrant parents, while consciously limiting television time that she feels promotes disrespect and materialism.

Anchors.tool_or_platform: Family rosary prayer booklet

09/08/1962.Event: On the parish’s annual feast of Our Lady’s Nativity procession, María volunteers to coordinate the bilingual hymns, discovering her dislike for microphones and public performance but her joy in supporting traditional Spanish hymns and processions from behind the scenes.

Anchors.tool_or_platform: Spanish hymn booklet "Cantos de María"

11/22/1963.Event: On the day news breaks of President John F. Kennedy’s assassination, María spends the evening in Adoration at St. Cecilia, turning to silent prayer for the nation and reaffirming her preference for contemplative response rather than heated political arguments that she hears on the radio.

Anchors.program_or_event: Impromptu Holy Hour for national mourning
Anchors.tool_or_platform: Parish AM radio broadcast

06/24/1965.Event: Following a minor but painful fall on the parish steps, María is diagnosed with early arthritis in her knees, beginning a long-term adjustment in which she walks more slowly, avoids long shopping trips she dislikes anyway, and offers her physical discomfort in prayer for priests and seminarians.

Anchors.tool_or_platform: Ibuprofen 200 mg over-the-counter regimen

03/15/1968.Event: As liturgical changes after the Second Vatican Council expand in her parish, María struggles with some of the newer music and informal styles of worship, but chooses to remain faithful and humble, intentionally seeking out the more traditional Latin chant Mass offered once a week.

Anchors.tool_or_platform: Parish Latin-English missalette

09/12/1970.Event: María begins a Saturday routine of lighting three votive candles for the intentions of priests, religious sisters, and seminarians, discovering a deep love for this quiet intercessory practice, even though she dislikes how crowded and noisy the parish vestibule can be before weekend Masses.

Anchors.tool_or_platform: Glass votive candles rack

09/19/1970.Event: María skips her usual Saturday votive candle routine and instead leaves the church immediately after Confession, feeling suddenly irritated by the flickering lights and the smell of candle wax that she finds overwhelming that evening.
09/19/1970.[Reasons of Change]: A vivid childhood memory of a house fire triggered by an overturned devotional candle resurfaces after she reads a local news article about a parish sacristy fire, making her temporarily uneasy around open flames despite decades of using votive candles peacefully.

09/20/1970.[Reasons of Change]: She overhears a shy teenage girl mention an interest in religious life and recognizes that the noisy vestibule is exactly where she can discreetly encourage vocations, prompting her to reframe the commotion as a field of quiet spiritual opportunities rather than an annoyance.

09/22/1970.Event: For the first time, María joins a parish folk Mass rehearsal in the church basement, agreeing to help with Spanish guitar hymns even though she has previously preferred traditional Latin chant and had avoided contemporary music groups.
09/22/1970.[Reasons of Change]: Her eldest grandson, recently confirmed, confesses that he only feels drawn to church when the music sounds like something his friends might listen to, leading María to see participation in the folk group as a sacrifice of her own preferences to help keep her grandchildren near the sacraments.

09/23/1970.Event: During her mid-morning routine at home, María begins listening to a daily religious talk radio program that analyzes current events and Church news, even though she had long avoided political and topical discussions in favor of silent contemplation.
09/23/1970.[Reasons of Change]: A priest friend suggests she offer more informed intercessory prayers for seminarians who are facing cultural pressures she has never encountered; he mails her a schedule of the radio program so she can better understand and pray about specific issues rather than general worries.

09/24/1970.Event: On Thursday afternoon, María deliberately turns off the religious talk radio program halfway through and spends an hour in complete silence with her rosary, deciding that the constant commentary is agitating her spirit more than helping her pray.
09/24/1970.[Reasons of Change]: A passing remark from the host about seminarians as "statistics" rather than souls deeply unsettles her, reminding her that she encounters them as individuals with letters, faces, and family stories, and she fears reducing them to abstract topics instead of loved persons she carries in her heart.

09/25/1970.Event: María spends her Friday morning carefully writing three long, encouraging letters to different seminarians, including personal anecdotes from her early years as a seamstress and mother, and walks slowly to the neighborhood post office to mail them.
09/25/1970.[Fact] Likes:: She enjoys detailed handwritten correspondence and sees letter-writing as a concrete way to offer emotional and spiritual support, preferring this quiet, reflective act over noisy parish committee meetings.

09/26/1970.Event: On Saturday, María reluctantly joins a crowded parish fundraising breakfast in the St. Cecilia school gym, discovering that she dislikes the loud clatter of dishes and constant announcements but appreciates meeting the parents of a new seminarian she has been praying for.
09/26/1970.[Fact] Dislikes:: She finds large, echoing indoor gatherings with amplified microphones draining and would rather spend that time in a quiet chapel or at home praying the rosary.

Anchors.program_or_event: Knights of Columbus Vocations Pancake Breakfast
Anchors.tool_or_platform: Battery-powered PA microphone system

09/27/1970.Event: After noticing increased stiffness in her knees throughout the week, María schedules a standing monthly appointment for gentle physical therapy, committing to exercises that will allow her to continue walking to daily Mass even though she dislikes medical visits.
09/27/1970.[Fact] Dislikes:: She has a long-standing discomfort with clinics and hospitals, associating them with worry and impersonal routines, but accepts them as necessary sacrifices to preserve her ability to participate in parish life.

Anchors.tool_or_platform: Set of 3 resistance bands for home knee exercises

09/28/1970.Event: María enrolls in a year-long diocesan correspondence course on Church history and the lives of saints, hoping to deepen the stories of perseverance she shares with her grandchildren and the seminarian she spiritually supports.
09/28/1970.[Fact] Likes:: She loves reading about the saints’ hardships and triumphs, seeing in them mirrors of her own family’s struggles and a storehouse of concrete examples to pass on in letters and conversations.

11/01/1971.Event: On All Saints’ Day, María accepts the parish music director’s request to cantor the Litany of the Saints at the main Mass, standing at the lectern with a microphone despite her long-standing discomfort with public performance, and leads the congregation through the names of over 40 saints.
11/01/1971.[Reasons of Change]: While reviewing her "Saints Through the Centuries" course, she notices how many hesitant, shy saints were pushed into public roles purely out of obedience; she feels personally addressed by a passage about Saint Zélie Martin’s timidity and concludes that her own trembling voice may be precisely the instrument God wishes to use for a seminarian in the pews who feels equally small.

11/15/1971.Event: At the annual Vocations Pancake Breakfast, María delivers a five-minute testimony using the PA system but discovers afterward that the amplified echo in the gym leaves her exhausted and unsettled, and she quietly tells the organizer she will not speak at future large-gym events.
11/15/1971.[Reasons of Change]: Hearing her own voice bounce back distorted from the metal rafters reminds her painfully of a childhood incident where her father’s angry shouting filled a similar echoing hall, and she realizes that this kind of space awakens old fears that interfere with the peace she wants to project when encouraging vocations.

12/08/1971.[Reasons of Change]: A seminarian sends her a sketch he drew of a single candle as a symbol of his vocation surviving in darkness; the drawing reminds her of how visual signs sustained her faith before she could read, convincing her that her earlier fear of open flames should be overcome so that children and seminarians can have the same strong, simple images that once protected her own belief.

01/06/1972.[Fact] Likes:: She enjoys teaching the faith woven naturally into family meals and conversation, preferring these informal story circles to structured talks, because she can see the questions and reactions on her grandchildren’s faces and respond tenderly.

11/22/1972.Event: On the anniversary of President Kennedy’s assassination, María consciously combines her old pattern of silent Adoration with her newer habit of being informed, bringing a clipped newspaper article about current political unrest to the chapel and placing it under her rosary as she prays for leaders and seminarians together.
11/22/1972.[Reasons of Change]: She recognizes that her early instinct to flee heated commentary left her uninformed, while her later embrace of talk radio risked drowning her prayer in opinion; by physically placing the article beneath her beads, she symbolically puts worldly turmoil "under" her intercession, satisfying her desire to know enough to pray concretely without letting events dominate her interior life.

05/18/2021.Event: Participated in a university mock trial circuit; disliked the theatrical performance emphasis and decided to avoid future mock-trial competitions.

02/25/2020.Event: Observed several full-day court dockets to study procedure but found the atmosphere exhausting and has limited future courtroom observation to targeted hearings.

07/30/2019.Event: Assisted a small business through a third-party compliance audit and disliked the volume of form-filling and checklist cross-referencing required.

01/15/2018.Event: Purchased a year-long CLE webinar bundle to earn credits but found the recorded lectures passive and did not renew the subscription.

09/05/2016.Event: Commissioned a forensic document analysis to verify a disputed signature and found the technical jargon-heavy reports difficult to translate for clients.

11/11/2014.Event: Took on a series of pro bono casework matters for asylum applicants but experienced burnout from high emotional demand and subsequently reduced pro bono intake.

06/22/2012.Event: Sat through a jury selection strategy seminar and disliked the reductive profiling exercises used to stratify potential jurors.

03/21/1955.Event: Tried courtroom sketching during an observation day but preferred taking structured notes and decided not to pursue sketching further.

08/09/2010.Event: Spent a year tracking proposed municipal legislation and found the slow-moving, politically-driven process frustrating, leading to a decision to limit time spent on legislative tracking.

01/20/2026.[Old Event]: Completed a three-day intensive legal research bootcamp focused on advanced database techniques and statutory history review.
01/20/2026.[Reasons of Change]: After a 48-hour silent residency that exposed an unusual sensory sensitivity to dense text blocks, prolonged case-law reading now triggers severe, disorienting headaches and a feeling of cognitive shutdown; to protect workflow and wellbeing they decided to delegate deep-dive research tasks.
01/20/2026.Event: Delegated a precedent-heavy assignment to a research assistant after experiencing sensory overload while skimming appellate opinions.

01/22/2026.[Old Event]: Spent weeks drafting a commercial contract for a vendor partnership and found the repetitive clause negotiation tedious; now prefers delegating routine contract drafting.
01/22/2026.[Reasons of Change]: Enrolled in a weekend calligraphy-and-structure workshop that reframed clause architecture as modular composition; the tactile, creative exercise unexpectedly rekindled satisfaction in crafting clean, elegant contractual language.
01/22/2026.Event: Drafted a modular revenue-share agreement using a new clause-library approach and enjoyed refining each provision's rhythm.

01/25/2026.[Old Event]: Volunteered at a weekend community legal clinic advising tenants on notice periods and documentation required for hearings.
01/25/2026.[Reasons of Change]: A recent clinic matter ended with a client evicted despite following advice; the resulting vivid dreams and guilt (a form of moral injury uncommon among peers) made the person step back to redesign intake and harm-minimization protocols before returning.
01/25/2026.Event: Declined the next clinic rotation and began drafting safer-clinic protocols to reduce risk of adverse outcomes for vulnerable clients.

02/01/2026.Event: Recorded a live Q&A legal podcast episode answering detailed tenant-rights questions and incorporated audience-submitted case summaries.

02/10/2026.[Old Event]: Purchased a year-long CLE webinar bundle to earn credits but found the recorded lectures passive and did not renew the subscription.
02/10/2026.[Reasons of Change]: A trusted childhood friend, now an improv-trained mediator, began delivering CLEs with interactive theater techniques and real-time role flips; the novel format transformed passive webinars into energizing skill labs.
02/10/2026.Event: Subscribed to an interactive CLE series that uses live improv to teach deposition techniques.

03/01/2026.[Old Event]: Participated in a university mock trial circuit; disliked the theatrical performance emphasis and decided to avoid future mock-trial competitions.
03/01/2026.[Reasons of Change]: After volunteering to coach a neurodiverse youth public-speaking group, the person discovered mock trials can be a structured, therapeutic rehearsal space for different cognition styles; that experience reframed mock trials as inclusive pedagogy rather than mere theatrics.
03/01/2026.Event: Founded a youth mock trial program tailored for neurodiverse learners and led the first weekend practice.

04/15/2026.Event: Contracted a forensic document specialist for a disputed signature but retained an independent plain-language summarizer to translate technical findings for clients, maintaining distance from the jargon-heavy reports.

06/30/2026.[Old Event]: Took on a series of pro bono casework matters for asylum applicants but experienced burnout from high emotional demand and subsequently reduced pro bono intake.
06/30/2026.[Reasons of Change]: Reconnected unexpectedly with a former pro bono client who recovered, became a community organizer, and invited the person into a mutual-aid collective with built-in peer support and rotating caseloads—this structural change made sustainable pro bono practice possible.
06/30/2026.Event: Reopened a limited pro bono roster focused on immigration cases with co-counsel and mandated peer-debriefs.

09/10/2026.[Old Event]: Sat through a jury selection strategy seminar and disliked the reductive profiling exercises used to stratify potential jurors.
09/10/2026.[Reasons of Change]: During a contemplative sabbatical in Iceland studying consensus-building among small fishing communities, the person became fascinated by how neutral questioning can foster representative juries rather than manipulate them; this academic interest changed their view of voir dire.
09/10/2026.Event: Led a pilot study testing neutral voir dire questions designed to improve jury representativeness.

12/12/2026.[Old Event]: Led a pilot study testing neutral voir dire questions designed to improve jury representativeness.
12/12/2026.[Reasons of Change]: After uncovering evidence that a private firm purchased the pilot's anonymized question-response patterns to micro-target jury pools in civil cases, the person felt ethically compromised and reversed course to oppose juror-targeting practices publicly.
12/12/2026.Event: Withdrew from the voir dire project and published an op-ed condemning commercial juror-targeting and advocating transparency.

12/15/2026.[Old Event]: Recorded a live Q&A legal podcast episode answering detailed tenant-rights questions and incorporated audience-submitted case summaries.
12/15/2026.[Reasons of Change]: A sophisticated deepfake episode appeared using a near-identical copy of their voice to give questionable legal advice; the surreal breach of vocal identity triggered a strong aversion to publishing audio and a determination to avoid amplifying misattributed speech.
12/15/2026.Event: Removed all public podcast episodes and halted recordings after confirming a deepfake used their vocal signature to impersonate them in a small-claims advisory recording.

12/17/2026.[Old Event]: Delegated a precedent-heavy assignment to a research assistant after experiencing sensory overload while skimming appellate opinions.
12/17/2026.[Reasons of Change]: Discovered an experimental audio-case-law service that presents cases via layered narrated summaries with paced pauses; the auditory format bypassed the sensory overload that had previously caused severe headaches, restoring joy in precedent work.
12/17/2026.Event: Subscribed to an audio-first case-law curator and completed a substantive memo using only narrated summaries and time-stamped citations.

12/18/2026.[Old Event]: Drafted a modular revenue-share agreement using a new clause-library approach and enjoyed refining each provision's rhythm.
12/18/2026.[Reasons of Change]: Learned that a widely used clause-library they relied on had been harvested, without consent, from small practices and used to train a commercial contract-mining AI that now undercuts boutique counsel; the ethical disquiet turned creative pleasure into reluctance.
12/18/2026.Event: Paused all contract drafting and initiated an audit of clause provenance before reusing shared libraries.

12/20/2026.[Old Event]: Declined the next clinic rotation and began drafting safer-clinic protocols to reduce risk of adverse outcomes for vulnerable clients.
12/20/2026.[Reasons of Change]: A former client returned with a hand-sewn banner celebrating a successful custody outcome and invited them to a restorative storytelling circle; the tangible, creative gratitude reopened a personal connection to direct service.
12/20/2026.Event: Rejoined the weekend clinic rotation after redesigning intake with harm-minimization protocols and witnessing a restored client's handmade 'case closed' banner.

12/22/2026.[Old Event]: Subscribed to an interactive CLE series that uses live improv to teach deposition techniques.
12/22/2026.[Reasons of Change]: Discovered the interactive CLE provider had repurposed anonymized attendee improv excerpts into a commercial training product without consent; feeling personally betrayed by a friend-instructor's involvement prompted withdrawal from webinar formats.
12/22/2026.Event: Unsubscribed from the interactive CLE series and filed an inquiry with the bar about consent and reuse of participant material.

01/02/2027.[Old Event]: Founded a youth mock trial program tailored for neurodiverse learners and led the first weekend practice.
01/02/2027.[Reasons of Change]: Program funders pushed to convert the inclusive mock trial into a competitive feeder for adult leagues, bringing pressure to standardize scoring and abandon neurodiversity accommodations; the person opposed the shift and chose to close the program rather than compromise its mission.
01/02/2027.Event: Closed the youth mock trial program and issued guidance on inclusive pedagogies for other community groups.

01/05/2027.[Old Event]: Reopened a limited pro bono roster focused on immigration cases with co-counsel and mandated peer-debriefs.
01/05/2027.[Reasons of Change]: A legal automation startup began offering near-free 'assistance' that siphoned straightforward matters away from in-person clinics and left the most complex, trauma-heavy matters to volunteers; the structural hollowing-out made sustaining pro bono emotionally and practically untenable.
01/05/2027.Event: Announced a temporary freeze on new pro bono intakes and convened local partners to redesign how automated tools intersect with volunteer legal aid.

01/08/2027.[Old Event]: Contracted a forensic document specialist for a disputed signature and found the technical jargon-heavy reports difficult to translate for clients.
01/08/2027.[Reasons of Change]: Met a forensic analyst who previously trained as a calligrapher and now produces AR-enhanced visualizations plus plain-language summaries; the combination finally made technical reports accessible and even intellectually engaging.
01/08/2027.Event: Commissioned an AR-enabled forensic analysis on a disputed deed and used the annotated visuals in a client-friendly briefing.

01/10/2027.[Old Event]: Withdrew from the voir dire project and published an op-ed condemning commercial juror-targeting and advocating transparency.
01/10/2027.[Reasons of Change]: After an investigative reporter exposed how commercial firms purchased anonymized voir dire datasets to skew pools, the person decided to engage rather than only protest—joining a transparency initiative to develop ethical voir dire tools to prevent abuse.
01/10/2027.Event: Joined a jury-transparency coalition as an ethics advisor to develop open-source voir dire templates that promote representativeness.

01/12/2027.[Old Event]: Spent a year tracking proposed municipal legislation and found the slow-moving, politically-driven process frustrating, leading to a decision to limit time spent on legislative tracking.
01/12/2027.[Reasons of Change]: Received an unexpected invitation to join a municipal advisory committee on tenant protections; participation surfaced rare backstage access to drafting windows where small edits yield outsized client impact, reframing legislative tracking as an effective leverage point.
01/12/2027.Event: Accepted a seat on a municipal advisory committee and began monitoring tenant-protection drafts closely to propose targeted amendments.

02/20/2027.[Old Event]: Removed all public podcast episodes and halted recordings after confirming a deepfake used their vocal signature to impersonate them in a small-claims advisory recording.
02/20/2027.[Reasons of Change]: A new voice-watermarking and provenance service (licensed to a local cooperative) provided cryptographic proof-of-origin for each episode; a hearing-impaired tenant group also urged revival because audio transcripts with time-aligned captions improved access. The combination of technical protection and demonstrable community benefit overcame prior aversion.
02/20/2027.Event: Resumed a weekly legal podcast using voice-watermarking; published an episode on tenant notice rights and directed ad revenue to the clinic fund.

03/05/2027.[Old Event]: Subscribed to an audio-first case-law curator and completed a substantive memo using only narrated summaries and time-stamped citations.
03/05/2027.[Reasons of Change]: Developed an unusual synesthetic reaction where statute and case names triggered strong olfactory hallucinations (an uncommon neurologic response after a cold-virus episode); the hallucinations became nauseating during sustained close reading, making dense precedent work intolerable and prompting a pivot to delegation.
03/05/2027.Event: Stopped a self-led precedent review midway through and contracted a legislative librarian to complete the research while redesigning workflows to rely on curated summaries.

04/10/2027.[Old Event]: Paused all contract drafting and initiated an audit of clause provenance before reusing shared libraries.
04/10/2027.[Reasons of Change]: Attended an experimental artisan typesetting and clause-craft retreat that treats clauses like short-form poetry; the tactile reformatting and micro-typography exercises reframed clause-drafting as craftsmanship rather than commodity.
04/10/2027.Event: Led a small project to redesign a standardized residential lease using modular, human-readable clauses and bespoke typography to reduce ambiguity.

05/15/2027.[Old Event]: Rejoined the weekend clinic rotation after redesigning intake with harm-minimization protocols and witnessing a restored client's handmade 'case closed' banner.
05/15/2027.[Reasons of Change]: Discovered that an intake spreadsheet (containing redacted but re-identifiable notes) was sold through a secondary data broker; one client's sketch—a tangible artifact—appeared in a resale listing. The concrete breach of trust produced a moral injury and a decision to pause direct clinic work until stronger data safeguards are in place.
05/15/2027.Event: Paused all clinic rotations, notified partner organizations about the suspected leak, and demanded audited data-protection practices before returning.

06/01/2027.Event: Declined an RSVP for a CLE webinar bundle after reviewing the platform's reuse clause that allowed recorded breakout content to be repurposed for commercial training without explicit participant consent.

08/01/2027.[Old Event]: Closed the youth mock trial program and issued guidance on inclusive pedagogies for other community groups.
08/01/2027.[Reasons of Change]: A former pro bono client who is now a CTO at an accessibility-focused VR startup offered unrestricted seed funding and bespoke VR courtroom environments that support sensory accommodations; the technology and aligned mission convinced them that mock trials could be relaunched without coercive competition pressures.
08/01/2027.Event: Accepted seed funding and reopened the neurodiverse youth mock trial program using VR courtrooms and trauma-informed facilitation.

09/15/2027.[Old Event]: Announced a temporary freeze on new pro bono intakes and convened local partners to redesign how automated tools intersect with volunteer legal aid.
09/15/2027.[Reasons of Change]: Co-designed a community-led intake filter (an 'ethical firewall') with civic technologists and survivor-advocates that routes straightforward matters to automated self-help while reserving complex, high-impact matters for supported volunteer counsel—this structural fix made sustainable pro bono feasible again.
09/15/2027.Event: Reopened a capped pro bono docket that uses the intake filter and mandatory peer-debriefs to protect volunteer wellbeing.

10/30/2027.[Old Event]: Commissioned an AR-enabled forensic analysis on a disputed deed and used the annotated visuals in a client-friendly briefing.
10/30/2027.[Reasons of Change]: A client experienced severe retraumatization when AR overlays replayed moments from an earlier contested case; additionally, the forensic vendor began licensing anonymized visualizations in a stock library without clear client consent. The combined harm and reuse of sensitive visualizations prompted withdrawal from that mode of evidence.
10/30/2027.Event: Terminated the relationship with the AR-enabled firm, secured deletion of client visuals, and mandated plain-language-only forensic reporting for future matters.

12/01/2027.Event: Hosted a two-day national workshop bringing judges, public defenders, and civil counsel together to pilot open-source voir dire templates focused on improving representativeness and reducing biased strikes.

01/08/2028.[Reasons of Change]: Discovered a pattern where draft redline snapshots from the advisory committee were being selectively leaked to a developer lobbyist who then pre-empted protective language; the realization that inside access was being weaponized against tenants caused a principled resignation and shift to external advocacy.
01/08/2028.Event: Resigned from the municipal advisory committee after evidence of leaks appeared and joined a civic watchdog to push for transparent drafting windows and public audit logs.
